1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## Tools & Requirements
     6→
     7→- **Node.js**: >=v24.13.0 (see `.node-version`, `.nvmrc`)
     8→- **npm**: >=11.0.0
     9→- **Core stack**: NestJS, Fastify, TypeScript (strict mode)
    10→- **Testing**: Jest (unit + e2e)
    11→- **Code quality**: ESLint v9+ (flat config), Prettier, Husky
    12→
    13→## Key Commands
    14→
    15→```bash
    16→# Development
    17→npm run start:dev          # Hot reload via webpack
    18→npm run start:debug        # Debug mode with hot reload
    19→npm run build              # Production build
    20→npm run start:prod         # Run production build
    21→
    22→# Testing
    23→npm run test               # Full suite (lint, format, type-check, unit, e2e)
    24→npm run test:unit          # Unit tests only
    25→npm run test:e2e           # E2E tests only
    26→npm run test:coverage      # Full suite with coverage reports
    27→npm run test:unit:coverage # Unit tests with coverage
    28→npm run test:e2e:coverage  # E2E tests with coverage
    29→
    30→# Code Quality
    31→npm run code-quality-check # lint + format + type-check
    32→npm run lint               # Check linting
    33→npm run lint:fix           # Auto-fix linting issues
    34→npm run format             # Prettier format
    35→npm run type-check         # TypeScript check without emit
    36→```
    37→
    38→## Architecture
    39→
    40→NestJS microservice with Fastify adapter, strict TypeScript, and modular domain organization.
    41→
    42→### Project Structure
    43→
    44→```
    45→src/
    46→├── modules/
    47→│   ├── app/              # Root application module
    48→│   ├── health/           # Health check endpoints
    49→│   └── env-config/       # Environment configuration & validation
    50→├── dto/                  # Data Transfer Objects
    51→├── utils/                # Utility functions
    52→├── middlewares/          # Custom middlewares
    53→├── interfaces/           # TypeScript interfaces
    54→├── decorators/           # Custom decorators
    55→├── interceptors/         # NestJS interceptors
    56→├── guards/               # Auth guards
    57→├── constants/            # Constants and enums
    58→└── configs/              # Configuration files
    59→test/                     # E2E tests
    60→```
    61→
    62→### Import Aliases (tsconfig.json)
    63→
    64→```typescript
    65→@config/*        → src/configs/*
    66→@constants/*     → src/constants/*
    67→@modules/*       → src/modules/*
    68→@utils/*         → src/utils/*
    69→@middlewares/*   → src/middlewares/*
    70→@services/*      → src/services/*
    71→@interfaces/*    → src/interfaces/*
    72→@decorators/*    → src/decorators/*
    73→@dto/*           → src/dto/*
    74→@guards/*        → src/guards/*
    75→@interceptors/*  → src/interceptors/*
    76→```
    77→
    78→## Development Rules
    79→
    80→### Code Style
    81→
    82→- **NO COMMENTS**, **NO EXPLANATIONS** in code (except ESLint disable commands)
    83→- **ALWAYS INSPECT EXISTING FILES** before creating new ones — match exact patterns
    84→- No `console.log` — use NestJS `Logger` instead
    85→- No relative imports — use `@aliases` only
    86→- All functions must have explicit return types
    87→- All custom functions must use a single object destructuring parameter (enforced by custom ESLint rule)
    88→
    89→### Environment Variables
    90→
    91→**ALWAYS follow this process when adding new environment variables:**
    92→
    93→1. Add to `.env`, `.env.test`, `.env.example`, `.env.test.example`
    94→2. Create constant in `src/constants/env-variables.constant.ts`: `export const MY_VAR = 'MY_VAR';`
    95→3. Add validation decorator in `src/configs/env-validations.config.ts`
    96→4. Add to loader in `src/utils/get-env-file.util.ts`
    97→5. Add test coverage in `test/env-config.module.e2e-spec.ts`
    98→
    99→**Never** access `process.env` directly — always use `EnvConfigService` or `getEnv` utility with constants.
   100→
   101→### Logging
   102→
   103→```typescript
   104→import { Logger } from '@nestjs/common';
   105→
   106→export class ClassName {
   107→  private readonly logger = new Logger(ClassName.name);
   108→}
   109→```
   110→
   111→### Common Patterns
   112→
   113→```typescript
   114→// Swagger decorators on controllers
   115→@ApiOperation({ summary: 'Description' })
   116→@ApiResponse({ status: HttpStatus.OK, description: 'Success' })
   117→```
   118→
   119→## ESLint Configuration
   120→
   121→- **No relative imports** (`no-restricted-imports` with `.*` pattern)
   122→- **Import order**: builtin → external → internal (`@aliases`) → type, alphabetized, newline between groups
   123→- **Relaxed rules** in `*.spec.ts`, `test/**`, `src/decorators/**`, `src/middlewares/**`, `src/interceptors/**`, `src/guards/**`
   124→
   125→## TypeScript Configuration
   126→
   127→- Target ES2023, strict mode with all checks enabled
   128→- `strictPropertyInitialization: false` (NestJS DI pattern)
   129→- `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` and `exactOptionalPropertyTypes` enabled — be precise with types
   130→
   131→## Testing
   132→
   133→- **Unit**: `*.unit-spec.ts` in `src/unit-tests/`
   134→- **E2E**: `*.e2e-spec.ts` in `test/`
   135→- **Coverage**: Separate reports in `coverage-unit/` and `coverage-e2e/`
   136→- Test files use `tsconfig.test.json` which relaxes strict checks
   137→- 95% minimum coverage across branches, functions, lines, statements
   138→

## Complete Backend Directory Exploration

### 1. Full Directory Structure

│   ├── modules/

│   │   │   └── app.module.ts          # Root application module
│   │   ├── env-config/

│   │   │   └── env-config.service.ts  # Service to access env vars
│   │   └── health/
│   │       ├── health.controller.ts   # Liveness/readiness endpoints
│   │       ├── health.module.ts       # Health check module
│   │       └── health.service.ts      # Health indicator service
│   ├── unit-tests/               # Unit test files (empty - .gitkeep only)

```

### 2. package.json Contents

**Scripts:**
- Development: `start:dev`, `start:debug`, `build`, `start:prod`
- Testing: `test`, `test:unit`, `test:e2e`, `test:coverage`, `test:unit:coverage`, `test:e2e:coverage`
- Code Quality: `lint`, `lint:fix`, `format`, `type-check`, `code-quality-check`

**Dependencies:**
- NestJS 11.1.16 (core, config, platform-fastify, axios, swagger, terminus)
- Fastify (via platform-fastify) with static files support
- TypeScript, class-validator, class-transformer
- RxJS for reactive patterns
- Axios for HTTP requests

**DevDependencies:**
- ESLint v9.31.0 (flat config), TypeScript ESLint

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Tools & Requirements

- **Node.js**: >=v24.13.0 (see `.node-version`, `.nvmrc`)
- **npm**: >=11.0.0
- **Core stack**: NestJS v11, Fastify, TypeScript (strict mode)
- **Testing**: Jest (unit + e2e)
- **Code quality**: ESLint v9+ (flat config), Prettier, Husky

## Key Commands

```bash
# Development
npm run start:dev          # Hot reload via webpack
npm run start:debug        # Debug mode with hot reload
npm run build              # Production build
npm run start:prod         # Run production build

# Testing
npm run test               # Full suite (lint, format, type-check, unit, e2e)
npm run test:unit          # Unit tests only
npm run test:e2e           # E2E tests only
npm run test:coverage      # Full suite with coverage reports
npm run test:unit:coverage # Unit tests with coverage
npm run test:e2e:coverage  # E2E tests with coverage

# Code Quality
npm run code-quality-check # lint + format + type-check
npm run lint               # Check linting
npm run lint:fix           # Auto-fix linting issues
npm run format             # Prettier format
npm run type-check         # TypeScript check without emit
```

## Architecture

NestJS microservice with Fastify adapter, strict TypeScript, and modular domain organization.

### Project Structure

```
src/
├── modules/
│   ├── app/              # Root application module
│   ├── health/           # Health check endpoints (liveness + readiness)
│   └── env-config/       # Environment configuration & validation
├── configs/              # Configuration files
│   └── env-validations.config.ts  # class-validator decorators for env vars
├── constants/            # Constants and enums
│   └── env-variables.constant.ts  # Env variable name string exports
├── dto/                  # Data Transfer Objects
├── utils/                # Utility functions
│   ├── common.util.ts           # Environment detection helpers
│   ├── get-env-file.util.ts     # Env file loader & validator
│   ├── get-env-variable.util.ts # getEnv standalone helper
│   └── setup-swagger.util.ts    # Swagger/OpenAPI setup
├── middlewares/          # Custom middlewares
├── interfaces/           # TypeScript interfaces
├── decorators/           # Custom decorators
├── interceptors/         # NestJS interceptors
├── guards/               # Auth guards
└── unit-tests/           # Unit tests (*.unit-spec.ts)
test/                     # E2E tests (*.e2e-spec.ts)
eslint-rules/             # Custom ESLint rules
└── require-object-params.js  # Enforces single object param rule
```

### Import Aliases (tsconfig.json)

```typescript
@config/*        → src/configs/*
@constants/*     → src/constants/*
@modules/*       → src/modules/*
@utils/*         → src/utils/*
@middlewares/*   → src/middlewares/*
@services/*      → src/services/*
@interfaces/*    → src/interfaces/*
@decorators/*    → src/decorators/*
@dto/*           → src/dto/*
@guards/*        → src/guards/*
@interceptors/*  → src/interceptors/*
```

## Development Rules

### Code Style

- **NO COMMENTS**, **NO EXPLANATIONS** in code (except ESLint disable commands)
- **ALWAYS INSPECT EXISTING FILES** before creating new ones — match exact patterns:
  - `.service.ts` → check similar modules
  - `.controller.ts` → check `/src/modules/`
  - `.dto.ts` → check `/src/dto/` or module folders
  - `.util.ts` → check `/src/utils/`
  - `.unit-spec.ts` → check `/src/unit-tests/`
  - `.e2e-spec.ts` → check `/test/`
  - `.interface.ts` → check `/src/interfaces/`
- No `console.log` — use NestJS `Logger` instead
- No relative imports — use `@aliases` only
- All functions must have explicit return types
- All custom functions must use a single object destructuring parameter (enforced by custom ESLint rule `require-object-params`)

### Environment Variables (CRITICAL)

**ALWAYS follow this complete process when adding new environment variables:**

#### Step 1: Add to Environment Files

Add to all four env files:

```bash
# .env, .env.example
NEW_VARIABLE_NAME=default_value   # Description of what this variable does
```

```bash
# .env.test, .env.test.example
NEW_VARIABLE_NAME=test_value
```

#### Step 2: Create Constant Export

Add to `src/constants/env-variables.constant.ts`:

```typescript
export const NEW_VARIABLE_NAME = 'NEW_VARIABLE_NAME';
```

#### Step 3: Add Validation Rule

Add to `src/configs/env-validations.config.ts` in the `EnvironmentVariables` class:

```typescript
@IsString()    // Use appropriate validator
@IsNotEmpty()
NEW_VARIABLE_NAME: string;
```

**Common validators:** `@IsString()`, `@IsNumber()`, `@IsBoolean()`, `@IsEnum(EnumType)`, `@IsNotEmpty()`, `@Min()`, `@Max()`, `@IsUrl()`

#### Step 4: Add to Environment Loader

Add to `src/utils/get-env-file.util.ts` in `getEnvVariables()`:

```typescript
NEW_VARIABLE_NAME: process.env['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME'],
```

#### Step 5: Add Test Coverage

Add to `test/env-config.module.e2e-spec.ts`:

```typescript
// In beforeAll hook
testEnvVariables['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME'] = String(envConfig['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME']);

// Test case
it('should load NEW_VARIABLE_NAME correctly', () => {
  expect(envConfigService.get<string>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME })).toBe(
    testEnvVariables['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME'],
  );
});
```

#### Usage: Two Approaches

**Approach 1: EnvConfigService (Dependency Injection)**

```typescript
import { EnvConfigService } from '@modules/env-config/env-config.service';
import { NEW_VARIABLE_NAME } from '@constants/env-variables.constant';

@Injectable()
export class MyService {
  constructor(private readonly configService: EnvConfigService) {}

  someMethod(): void {
    const value = this.configService.get<string>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME });
    const numericValue = this.configService.get<number>({
      key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME,
      converter: Number,
    });
  }
}
```

**Approach 2: getEnv Utility (Standalone)**

```typescript
import { getEnv } from '@utils/get-env-variable.util';
import { NEW_VARIABLE_NAME } from '@constants/env-variables.constant';

const value = getEnv<string>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME });
const numericValue = getEnv<number>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME, converter: Number });
```

**Important:**
- **ALWAYS** complete ALL 5 steps — missing any step causes validation failures
- **NEVER** access `process.env` directly in application code
- **ALWAYS** use constants from `@constants/env-variables.constant`

### Logging

```typescript
import { Logger } from '@nestjs/common';

export class ClassName {
  private readonly logger = new Logger(ClassName.name);
}
```

### Common Patterns

```typescript
// Swagger decorators on controllers
@ApiOperation({ summary: 'Description' })
@ApiResponse({ status: HttpStatus.OK, description: 'Success' })
```

## ESLint Configuration

- **No relative imports** (`no-restricted-imports` with `.*` pattern)
- **No console** — use Logger
- **Explicit return types** on all functions (`@typescript-eslint/explicit-module-boundary-types`)
- **Import order**: builtin → external → internal (`@aliases`) → type, alphabetized, newline between groups
- **Custom rule** `require-object-params`: all custom functions must use a single object destructuring parameter
- **Relaxed rules** in `*.spec.ts`, `test/**`, `src/decorators/**`, `src/middlewares/**`, `src/interceptors/**`, `src/guards/**`

## TypeScript Configuration

- Target ES2023, strict mode with all checks enabled
- `strictPropertyInitialization: false` (NestJS DI pattern)
- `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`, `noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature` enabled — be precise with types
- Incremental compilation enabled for faster builds
- Separate `tsconfig.test.json` relaxes strict checks for test files

## Testing

- **Unit**: `*.unit-spec.ts` in `src/unit-tests/`
- **E2E**: `*.e2e-spec.ts` in `test/`
- **Coverage**: Separate reports in `coverage-unit/` and `coverage-e2e/`
- Test files use `tsconfig.test.json` which relaxes strict checks
- **95% minimum coverage** across branches, functions, lines, statements (enforced in `test/jest.coverage.config.ts`)

## Key Dependencies

### Core

- **@nestjs/\*** — NestJS framework and modules
- **@nestjs/platform-fastify** — Fastify adapter for performance
- **@nestjs/terminus** — Health check module
- **@nestjs/swagger** — OpenAPI/Swagger documentation

### Validation & Transform

- **class-validator** + **class-transformer** — DTO validation and transformation

### Utilities

- **rxjs** — Reactive patterns
- **axios** / **@nestjs/axios** — HTTP requests

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project Structure

Monorepo with two independent workspaces — each has its own `package.json` and `node_modules`:

```
backend/    # NestJS + Fastify API (see backend/CLAUDE.md)
frontend/   # React app (see frontend/CLAUDE.md — not yet scaffolded)
```

Root `package.json` orchestrates both workspaces via `npm --prefix` delegates.

## Root Commands

```bash
# Install
npm run install:all      # Install deps in both backend and frontend

# Development
npm run backend:dev      # Start backend dev server
npm run frontend:dev     # Start frontend dev server
npm run dev              # Start both concurrently

# Build & Test
npm run build            # Build both (backend then frontend)
npm run test             # Test both sequentially
npm run lint             # Lint both

# Backend-specific shortcuts from root
npm run backend:build
npm run backend:test
npm run backend:lint
npm run backend:lint:fix
npm run backend:format
npm run backend:type-check
```

## Stack

- **Backend**: NestJS v11, Fastify, TypeScript strict — see `backend/CLAUDE.md`
- **Frontend**: React — see `frontend/CLAUDE.md` (not yet scaffolded)
- **Node.js**: >=24.13.0

## Workspace Notes

- Each workspace is fully independent — run `npm install` inside `backend/` or `frontend/` separately, or use `npm run install:all` from root
- No npm workspaces hoisting — `node_modules` lives inside each workspace directory
- `.env` files are gitignored in each workspace; use `.env.example` as reference
- Git workflow: branch naming, commit style, and PR conventions are defined per workspace in their respective `CLAUDE.md`

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project Structure

Monorepo with two independent workspaces — each has its own `package.json` and `node_modules`:

```
backend/    # NestJS + Fastify API (see backend/CLAUDE.md)
frontend/   # React app (see frontend/CLAUDE.md)
```

Root `package.json` orchestrates both workspaces via `npm --prefix` delegates.

## Root Commands

```bash
npm run install:all      # Install deps in both backend and frontend

npm run backend:dev      # Start backend dev server
npm run frontend:dev     # Start frontend dev server
npm run dev              # Start both concurrently

npm run build            # Build both (backend then frontend)
npm run test             # Test both sequentially
npm run lint             # Lint both

# Backend-specific shortcuts from root
npm run backend:build
npm run backend:test
npm run backend:lint
npm run backend:lint:fix
npm run backend:format
npm run backend:type-check
```

## Stack

- **Backend**: NestJS v11, Fastify, TypeScript strict — see `backend/CLAUDE.md`
- **Frontend**: React — see `frontend/CLAUDE.md` (not yet scaffolded)
- **Node.js**: >=24.13.0

## Workspace Notes

- Each workspace is fully independent — run `npm install` inside `backend/` or `frontend/` separately, or use `npm run install:all` from root
- No npm workspaces hoisting — `node_modules` lives inside each workspace directory
- `.env` files are gitignored in each workspace; use `.env.example` as reference

input.content:
# Backend

NestJS backend service for the webapp monorepo.

## Tech Stack

- **Runtime**: Node.js >= 24.13.0
- **Framework**: NestJS v11 + Fastify
- **Language**: TypeScript (strict mode)
- **Testing**: Jest (unit + e2e)
- **Documentation**: Swagger/OpenAPI

## Getting Started

```bash
# Install dependencies
npm install

# Copy env files
cp .env.example .env
cp .env.test.example .env.test

# Start dev server with hot reload
npm run start:dev
```

Swagger available at `http://localhost:9069/api/swagger` during development.

## Key Commands

```bash
npm run start:dev             # Dev server with hot reload
npm run build                 # Production build
npm run test                  # Full test suite (lint + format + types + unit + e2e)
npm run test:unit             # Unit tests
npm run test:e2e              # E2E tests
npm run code-quality-check    # Lint + format + type-check
```

Full command list in `package.json`.

## Architecture

```
src/
├── modules/          # Feature modules organized by domain
│   ├── app/          # Root application module
│   ├── health/       # Liveness & readiness endpoints
│   └── env-config/   # Environment config & validation
├── configs/          # Configuration (env validation rules)
├── constants/        # Env variable name constants
├── dto/              # Data Transfer Objects
├── utils/            # Utility functions
├── interfaces/       # TypeScript interfaces
├── decorators/       # Custom decorators
├── interceptors/     # NestJS interceptors
├── middlewares/      # Custom middlewares
├── guards/           # Auth guards
└── unit-tests/       # Unit tests (*.unit-spec.ts)
test/                 # E2E tests (*.e2e-spec.ts)
```

See [CLAUDE.md](./CLAUDE.md) for detailed development guidelines including import aliases, env variable workflow, ESLint rules, and coding conventions.

# Backend

NestJS backend service for the webapp monorepo.

## Tech Stack

- **Runtime**: Node.js >= 24.13.0
- **Framework**: NestJS + Fastify
- **Language**: TypeScript (strict mode)
- **Testing**: Jest (unit + e2e)
- **Documentation**: Swagger/OpenAPI

## Getting Started

```bash
# Install dependencies
npm install

# Copy env files
cp .env.example .env
cp .env.test.example .env.test

# Start dev server with hot reload
npm run start:dev
```

Swagger available at `http://localhost:3000/swagger` during development.

## Key Commands

```bash
npm run start:dev             # Dev server with hot reload
npm run build                 # Production build
npm run test                  # Full test suite (lint + format + types + unit + e2e)
npm run test:unit             # Unit tests
npm run test:e2e              # E2E tests
npm run code-quality-check    # Lint + format + type-check
```

Full command list in `package.json`.

## Architecture

```
src/
├── modules/          # Feature modules organized by domain
├── dto/              # Data Transfer Objects
├── utils/            # Utility functions
├── configs/          # Configuration files
└── constants/        # App constants and env variable names
test/                 # E2E tests
```

See [CLAUDE.md](./CLAUDE.md) for detailed development guidelines including import aliases, env variable workflow, and coding conventions.

content.text:
All three files updated:

**`backend/CLAUDE.md`** — expanded significantly:
- Added actual directory structure with file descriptions (not just folder stubs)
- Full 5-step env var process with concrete code examples (matching recommendations-service style)
- Both usage approaches for env vars (DI + standalone)
- Detailed ESLint rules section including the custom `require-object-params` rule
- Added file pattern matching guide for new files
- Expanded TypeScript config notes
- Key dependencies section

**`CLAUDE.md`** (root) — minor cleanup:
- Clarified frontend is not yet scaffolded
- Added note about git workflow being defined per workspace

**`backend/README.md`** — updated:
- Fixed Swagger URL to match actual port/prefix from `.env.example` (`9069/api/swagger`)
- Expanded directory structure to match actual `src/` layout
- Added links section pointing to CLAUDE.md

1→# E2E Tests
     2→
     3→## Location & Naming
     4→
     5→- Files: `*.e2e-spec.ts` in `test/`
     6→- Run: `npm run test:e2e`
     7→- Coverage: `npm run test:e2e:coverage`
     8→- DB reset: `npm run db:reset:test` (runs automatically via `test:e2e`)
     9→
    10→## Scope
    11→
    12→Test these:
    13→- REST endpoints via real HTTP requests (supertest)
    14→- Full module integration (controller → service → DB)
    15→- Database read/write operations
    16→- Cache behavior
    17→- Controller method dispatch (without HTTP server)
    18→- Service logic with mocked dependencies
    19→
    20→Do NOT e2e test:
    21→- Pure utility logic (covered by unit tests)
    22→- Internal method logic (covered by unit tests)
    23→
    24→## App Bootstrap Pattern (HTTP Tests)
    25→
    26→```typescript
    27→import { INestApplication } from '@nestjs/common';
    28→import { Test, TestingModule } from '@nestjs/testing';
    29→import * as request from 'supertest';
    30→import { App } from 'supertest/types';
    31→
    32→import { CacheModule } from '@modules/cache/cache.module';
    33→import { EnvConfigModule } from '@modules/env-config/env-config.module';
    34→import { CorrelationIdMiddleware } from '../src/middlewares/correlation-id.middleware';
    35→
    36→describe('FeatureName API Integration Tests', () => {
    37→  let app: INestApplication;
    38→  let getHttpServer: App;
    39→
    40→  beforeAll(async () => {
    41→    const moduleFixture: TestingModule = await Test.createTestingModule({
    42→      imports: [EnvConfigModule, CacheModule],
    43→      controllers: [FeatureController],
    44→      providers: [
    45→        FeatureService,
    46→        {
    47→          provide: ExternalService,
    48→          useValue: { method: jest.fn() },
    49→        },
    50→      ],
    51→    }).compile();
    52→
    53→    app = moduleFixture.createNestApplication();
    54→
    55→    const correlationIdMiddleware = new CorrelationIdMiddleware();
    56→    app.use(correlationIdMiddleware.use.bind(correlationIdMiddleware));
    57→
    58→    getHttpServer = app.getHttpServer() as App;
    59→
    60→    await app.init();
    61→  });
    62→
    63→  afterAll(async () => {
    64→    await app.close();
    65→  });
    66→});
    67→```
    68→
    69→## Making HTTP Requests
    70→
    71→```typescript
    72→const response = await request(getHttpServer)
    73→  .get('/api/v1/endpoint')
    74→  .query({ param: 'value' });
    75→
    76→expect(response.status).toBe(200);
    77→expect(response.body).toMatchObject({ expected: 'shape' });
    78→```
    79→
    80→## Controller Tests (Without HTTP Server)
    81→
    82→Some e2e tests test controllers directly without spinning up an HTTP server:
    83→
    84→```typescript
    85→describe('FeatureController', () => {
    86→  let controller: FeatureController;
    87→  let mockService: jest.Mocked<FeatureService>;
    88→
    89→  beforeEach(async () => {
    90→    mockService = {
    91→      getData: jest.fn(),
    92→    } as unknown as jest.Mocked<FeatureService>;
    93→
    94→    const module: TestingModule = await Test.createTestingModule({
    95→      controllers: [FeatureController],
    96→      providers: [{ provide: FeatureService, useValue: mockService }],
    97→    }).compile();
    98→
    99→    controller = module.get<FeatureController>(FeatureController);
   100→  });
   101→
   102→  afterEach(() => {
   103→    jest.clearAllMocks();
   104→  });
   105→});
   106→```
   107→
   108→## Mocking Strategy
   109→
   110→Two approaches used in this codebase:
   111→
   112→1. **Inline mock providers** (most common): Pass `{ provide: X, useValue: mockObj }` in the `providers` array.
   113→2. **`.overrideProvider()`**: Chain on `Test.createTestingModule({...}).overrideProvider(X).useValue(mockObj).compile()`.
   114→
   115→Mock all external services (Algolia, MMS, OpenAI, AWS). Use real DB (test DB is reset before suite). Use real `CacheModule` for cache integration tests; spy on `CacheService` methods as needed.
   116→
   117→Apply `CorrelationIdMiddleware` manually after `createNestApplication()` for HTTP tests. Add `ValidationPipe` when the endpoint uses DTO validation.
   118→
   119→## Coverage Requirements
   120→
   121→95% minimum across branches, functions, lines, and statements.
   122→Coverage is collected only from `*.(controller|service).ts` files.
   123→Configured in `test/jest-e2e.coverage.config.ts` (extends `test/jest.coverage.config.ts`).
   124→Run `npm run test:e2e:coverage` to verify before committing.
   125→
   126→Excluded from coverage:
   127→- `**/load-test/**`
   128→- `src/modules/newrelic/newrelic.service.ts`
   129→- `src/modules/rest/related-products/algolia.service.ts`
   130→- `src/modules/rest/related-products/mms.service.ts`
   131→- `src/modules/bigquery/**`
   132→- `src/modules/cli/**`
   133→- `src/modules/db/style-copurchases/style-copurchases.service.ts`
   134→- `src/modules/color-match-worker/color-match-worker.service.ts`
   135→
   136→## Database
   137→
   138→Test DB is MySQL replica. Reset runs automatically before e2e suite.
   139→For manual reset: `npm run db:reset:test`.
   140→Never use production DB for tests.
   141→

1→# Unit Tests
     2→
     3→## Location & Naming
     4→
     5→- Files: `*.unit-spec.ts` in `src/unit-tests/`
     6→- Run: `npm run test:unit`
     7→- Coverage: `npm run test:unit:coverage`
     8→
     9→## Scope
    10→
    11→Test these:
    12→- Utility functions (`src/utils/`)
    13→- Service layer methods (`src/modules/**/*.service.ts`)
    14→- Decorators (`src/decorators/`)
    15→- Model methods (`src/modules/db/**/models/*.model.ts`)
    16→- Config files (`src/configs/`)
    17→
    18→Do NOT unit test:
    19→- Controllers (covered by e2e tests)
    20→- Full module integration (covered by e2e tests)
    21→- Third-party integrations (mock at boundary)
    22→
    23→## Mocking Pattern
    24→
    25→Always use `Test.createTestingModule` and provide mocks via `useValue`. Never instantiate real services.
    26→
    27→```typescript
    28→import { Test, TestingModule } from '@nestjs/testing';
    29→
    30→const module: TestingModule = await Test.createTestingModule({
    31→  providers: [
    32→    ServiceUnderTest,
    33→    {
    34→      provide: DependencyService,
    35→      useValue: { methodName: jest.fn() },
    36→    },
    37→    {
    38→      provide: KNEX_READ_CONNECTION,
    39→      useValue: mockKnexRead,
    40→    },
    41→    {
    42→      provide: KNEX_WRITE_CONNECTION,
    43→      useValue: mockKnexWrite,
    44→    },
    45→  ],
    46→}).compile();
    47→```
    48→
    49→Use `jest.Mocked<T>` for typed mocks:
    50→
    51→```typescript
    52→let service: jest.Mocked<DependencyService>;
    53→service = { methodName: jest.fn() } as jest.Mocked<DependencyService>;
    54→```
    55→
    56→Always `jest.clearAllMocks()` in `afterEach`.
    57→
    58→## Test Structure
    59→
    60→```typescript
    61→describe('ClassName', () => {
    62→  let subject: SubjectClass;
    63→  let mockDep: jest.Mocked<DepClass>;
    64→
    65→  beforeEach(async () => {
    66→    // set up mocks and module
    67→  });
    68→
    69→  afterEach(() => {
    70→    jest.clearAllMocks();
    71→  });
    72→
    73→  describe('methodName', () => {
    74→    it('should [expected behavior] when [condition]', async () => {
    75→      // arrange
    76→      // act
    77→      // assert
    78→    });
    79→  });
    80→});
    81→```
    82→
    83→Test every meaningful branch: happy path, error path, edge cases (null, empty, boundary values).
    84→
    85→## Coverage Requirements (STRICT — NON-NEGOTIABLE)
    86→
    87→**95% minimum across ALL metrics: branches, functions, lines, statements.**
    88→
    89→This threshold is enforced by Jest and will fail CI if not met. Every new test file you write must meet this bar. When writing tests:
    90→
    91→- Cover every branch: `if/else`, ternaries, optional chaining fallbacks, early returns
    92→- Cover every error path: thrown exceptions, rejected promises, caught errors
    93→- Cover edge cases: `null`, `undefined`, empty arrays/strings, boundary numeric values
    94→- Do not write tests that trivially pass without asserting meaningful behavior
    95→
    96→`/modules/db/` patterns are excluded from unit coverage config, but all other code must meet 95%.
    97→
    98→Run `npm run test:unit:coverage` to verify before committing. Never commit tests that cause coverage to drop below 95%.
    99→
   100→## Proactive Coverage Improvement
   101→
   102→When working on tests, **always check overall coverage first**:
   103→
   104→```bash
   105→npm run test:unit:coverage
   106→```
   107→
   108→If any file or suite is below 95% on any metric, treat it as a bug. Proactively identify and fix the lowest-coverage test suites, especially those that drag down the overall score. Do not limit yourself to only the file you were asked to test — if a neighboring suite is at 60% branches, fix it.
   109→
   110→Prioritize fixing suites with:
   111→- Low branch coverage (missed `if/else` paths, uncaught error branches)
   112→- Missing error-path tests
   113→- Large uncovered utility or service files
   114→
   115→After adding tests to a failing suite, re-run coverage to confirm the improvement before moving on.
   116→
   117→## Logger Spying
   118→
   119→Spy on Logger prototype to assert log calls without noise:
   120→
   121→```typescript
   122→loggerErrorSpy = jest.spyOn(Logger.prototype, 'error').mockImplementation(() => undefined);
   123→loggerDebugSpy = jest.spyOn(Logger.prototype, 'debug').mockImplementation(() => undefined);
   124→loggerWarnSpy = jest.spyOn(Logger.prototype, 'warn').mockImplementation(() => undefined);
   125→```
   126→

content.text:
Perfect! I have successfully read all the requested files from the backend directory. Here's a complete summary:

## Files Read

### 1. **package.json**
Contains backend dependencies (NestJS v11, Fastify, class-validator, dotenv) and scripts for development, testing, and code quality checks.

### 2. **tsconfig.test.json**
Extends base tsconfig but relaxes strict type checking for test files (noImplicitAny, strictNullChecks, strictPropertyInitialization, noImplicitReturns, noFallthroughCasesInSwitch all set to false).

### 3. **test/ directory**
Contains only `.gitkeep` file (no actual test files in this directory yet).

### 4. **health.controller.ts**
NestJS controller with two endpoints:
- `GET /liveness` - Simple liveness probe returning `{ status: 'up' }`
- `GET /readiness` - Readiness probe checking app health and memory heap, handles SIGTERM for graceful shutdown

### 5. **health.service.ts**
Injectable service that checks app health, logs the check, and returns uptime and timestamp.

### 6. **health.module.ts**
NestJS module that imports TerminusModule with graceful shutdown configuration and HttpModule, exposes HealthController and HealthService.

### 7. **env-config.service.ts**
Injectable service that wraps NestJS ConfigService to fetch environment variables with optional converters, throws error if variable is undefined.

### 8. **env-config.module.ts**
NestJS module that imports ConfigModule with `loadEnvVariables()` factory and exports EnvConfigService.

### 9. **common.util.ts**
Utility functions for environment detection: `isTestEnv()`, `isProdEnv()`, `isStagingEnv()`, `isDevEnv()`.

### 10. **get-env-variable.util.ts**
Standalone helper `getEnv()` that instantiates ConfigService and EnvConfigService to fetch environment variables without dependency injection.

### 11. **get-env-file.util.ts**
Loads and validates environment variables:
- `getEnvFile()` - Returns '.env.test' for test env, '.env' otherwise
- `getEnvVariables()` - Returns object with all environment variables from process.env
- `loadEnvVariables()` - ConfigFactory that loads .env file for dev/test, validates, returns factory function

### 12. **setup-swagger.util.ts**
Sets up Swagger/OpenAPI documentation builder with title, description, version, bearer auth, and mounts at `{globalPrefix}/swagger`.

### 13. **main.ts**
Bootstrap function that:
- Creates NestFastifyApplication with Fastify adapter
- Enables CORS and global ValidationPipe
- Sets global prefix (excluding liveness/readiness)
- Sets up Swagger
- Listens on port from env config
- Handles hot module replacement for development

### 14. **env-variables.interface.ts**
Enum defining four environments: DEVELOPMENT, STAGING, PRODUCTION, TEST.

### 15. **env-validations.config.ts**
Class-validator decorators for environment variables:
- `NODE_ENV` (enum)
- `PORT` (number, 0-65535)
- `GLOBAL_PREFIX` (string, not empty)
- `SERVER_NAME` (string, not empty)
- `IS_COMPACT_LOGS` (boolean)
- `IS_JSON_LIKE_LOGS` (boolean)
- `GRACEFUL_SHUTDOWN_DELAY` (number, 0-60000)

Includes `validate()` function that validates config using plainToInstance and validateSync.

### 16. **env-variables.constant.ts**
String constant exports for all environment variable names.

### 17. **.env.test.example**
Test environment configuration with default values (port 3001, test server name, compact logs enabled, etc.).

All files are now available for your review at the absolute paths in `/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/no-name-proj/backend/`.

	"scripts": {
		"build": "nest build",
		"format": "prettier --write \"src/**/*.ts\" \"test/**/*.ts\"",
		"type-check": "tsc --noEmit",
		"start": "nest start",
		"start:dev": "nest start --watch --webpack --webpackPath webpack.config.js",
		"start:debug": "nest start --debug --watch --webpack --webpackPath webpack.config.js",
		"start:prod": "node dist/main",
		"lint": "eslint \"{src,apps,libs,test}/**/*.ts\"",
		"lint:fix": "eslint \"{src,apps,libs,test}/**/*.ts\" --fix",
		"code-quality-check": "npm run lint && npm run format && npm run type-check",
		"test:unit": "dotenv -e .env.test -- npx jest --config ./test/jest-unit.config.ts",
		"test:e2e": "dotenv -e .env.test -- npx jest --runInBand --forceExit --config ./test/jest-e2e.config.ts",
		"test:unit:coverage": "dotenv -e .env.test -- npx jest --config ./test/jest-unit.coverage.config.ts",

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Tools & Requirements

- **Node.js**: >=v24.13.0 (see `.node-version`, `.nvmrc`)
- **npm**: >=11.0.0
- **Core stack**: NestJS v11, Fastify, TypeScript (strict mode)
- **Testing**: Jest (unit + e2e)
- **Code quality**: ESLint v9+ (flat config), Prettier, Husky

## Key Commands

```bash
# Development
npm run start:dev          # Hot reload via webpack
npm run start:debug        # Debug mode with hot reload
npm run build              # Production build
npm run start:prod         # Run production build

# Testing
npm run test               # Full suite (lint, format, type-check, unit, e2e)
npm run test:unit          # Unit tests only
npm run test:e2e           # E2E tests only
npm run test:coverage      # Full suite with coverage reports
npm run test:unit:coverage # Unit tests with coverage
npm run test:e2e:coverage  # E2E tests with coverage

# Code Quality
npm run code-quality-check # lint + format + type-check
npm run lint               # Check linting
npm run lint:fix           # Auto-fix linting issues
npm run format             # Prettier format
npm run type-check         # TypeScript check without emit
```

## Architecture

NestJS microservice with Fastify adapter, strict TypeScript, and modular domain organization.

### Project Structure

```
src/
├── modules/
│   ├── app/              # Root application module
│   ├── health/           # Health check endpoints (liveness + readiness)
│   └── env-config/       # Environment configuration & validation
├── configs/              # Configuration files
│   └── env-validations.config.ts  # class-validator decorators for env vars
├── constants/            # Constants and enums
│   └── env-variables.constant.ts  # Env variable name string exports
├── dto/                  # Data Transfer Objects
├── utils/                # Utility functions
│   ├── common.util.ts           # Environment detection helpers
│   ├── get-env-file.util.ts     # Env file loader & validator
│   ├── get-env-variable.util.ts # getEnv standalone helper
│   └── setup-swagger.util.ts    # Swagger/OpenAPI setup
├── middlewares/          # Custom middlewares
├── interfaces/           # TypeScript interfaces
├── decorators/           # Custom decorators
├── interceptors/         # NestJS interceptors
├── guards/               # Auth guards
└── unit-tests/           # Unit tests (*.unit-spec.ts)
test/                     # E2E tests (*.e2e-spec.ts)
eslint-rules/             # Custom ESLint rules
└── require-object-params.js  # Enforces single object param rule
```

### Import Aliases (tsconfig.json)

```typescript
@config/*        → src/configs/*
@constants/*     → src/constants/*
@modules/*       → src/modules/*
@utils/*         → src/utils/*
@middlewares/*   → src/middlewares/*
@services/*      → src/services/*
@interfaces/*    → src/interfaces/*
@decorators/*    → src/decorators/*
@dto/*           → src/dto/*
@guards/*        → src/guards/*
@interceptors/*  → src/interceptors/*
```

## Development Rules

### Code Style

- **NO COMMENTS**, **NO EXPLANATIONS** in code (except ESLint disable commands)
- **ALWAYS INSPECT EXISTING FILES** before creating new ones — match exact patterns:
  - `.service.ts` → check similar modules
  - `.controller.ts` → check `/src/modules/`
  - `.dto.ts` → check `/src/dto/` or module folders
  - `.util.ts` → check `/src/utils/`
  - `.unit-spec.ts` → check `/src/unit-tests/`
  - `.e2e-spec.ts` → check `/test/`
  - `.interface.ts` → check `/src/interfaces/`
- No `console.log` — use NestJS `Logger` instead
- No relative imports — use `@aliases` only
- All functions must have explicit return types
- All custom functions must use a single object destructuring parameter (enforced by custom ESLint rule `require-object-params`)

### Environment Variables (CRITICAL)

**ALWAYS follow this complete process when adding new environment variables:**

#### Step 1: Add to Environment Files

Add to all four env files:

```bash
# .env, .env.example
NEW_VARIABLE_NAME=default_value   # Description of what this variable does
```

```bash
# .env.test, .env.test.example
NEW_VARIABLE_NAME=test_value
```

#### Step 2: Create Constant Export

Add to `src/constants/env-variables.constant.ts`:

```typescript
export const NEW_VARIABLE_NAME = 'NEW_VARIABLE_NAME';
```

#### Step 3: Add Validation Rule

Add to `src/configs/env-validations.config.ts` in the `EnvironmentVariables` class:

```typescript
@IsString()    // Use appropriate validator
@IsNotEmpty()
NEW_VARIABLE_NAME: string;
```

**Common validators:** `@IsString()`, `@IsNumber()`, `@IsBoolean()`, `@IsEnum(EnumType)`, `@IsNotEmpty()`, `@Min()`, `@Max()`, `@IsUrl()`

#### Step 4: Add to Environment Loader

Add to `src/utils/get-env-file.util.ts` in `getEnvVariables()`:

```typescript
NEW_VARIABLE_NAME: process.env['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME'],
```

#### Step 5: Add Test Coverage

Add to `test/env-config.module.e2e-spec.ts`:

```typescript
// In beforeAll hook
testEnvVariables['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME'] = String(envConfig['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME']);

// Test case
it('should load NEW_VARIABLE_NAME correctly', () => {
  expect(envConfigService.get<string>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME })).toBe(
    testEnvVariables['NEW_VARIABLE_NAME'],
  );
});
```

#### Usage: Two Approaches

**Approach 1: EnvConfigService (Dependency Injection)**

```typescript
import { EnvConfigService } from '@modules/env-config/env-config.service';
import { NEW_VARIABLE_NAME } from '@constants/env-variables.constant';

@Injectable()
export class MyService {
  constructor(private readonly configService: EnvConfigService) {}

  someMethod(): void {
    const value = this.configService.get<string>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME });
    const numericValue = this.configService.get<number>({
      key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME,
      converter: Number,
    });
  }
}
```

**Approach 2: getEnv Utility (Standalone)**

```typescript
import { getEnv } from '@utils/get-env-variable.util';
import { NEW_VARIABLE_NAME } from '@constants/env-variables.constant';

const value = getEnv<string>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME });
const numericValue = getEnv<number>({ key: NEW_VARIABLE_NAME, converter: Number });
```

**Important:**
- **ALWAYS** complete ALL 5 steps — missing any step causes validation failures
- **NEVER** access `process.env` directly in application code
- **ALWAYS** use constants from `@constants/env-variables.constant`

### Logging

```typescript
import { Logger } from '@nestjs/common';

export class ClassName {
  private readonly logger = new Logger(ClassName.name);
}
```

### Common Patterns

```typescript
// Swagger decorators on controllers
@ApiOperation({ summary: 'Description' })
@ApiResponse({ status: HttpStatus.OK, description: 'Success' })
```

## ESLint Configuration

- **No relative imports** (`no-restricted-imports` with `.*` pattern)
- **No console** — use Logger
- **Explicit return types** on all functions (`@typescript-eslint/explicit-module-boundary-types`)
- **Import order**: builtin → external → internal (`@aliases`) → type, alphabetized, newline between groups
- **Custom rule** `require-object-params`: all custom functions must use a single object destructuring parameter
- **Relaxed rules** in `*.spec.ts`, `test/**`, `src/decorators/**`, `src/middlewares/**`, `src/interceptors/**`, `src/guards/**`

## TypeScript Configuration

- Target ES2023, strict mode with all checks enabled
- `strictPropertyInitialization: false` (NestJS DI pattern)
- `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`, `noPropertyAccessFromIndexSignature` enabled — be precise with types
- Incremental compilation enabled for faster builds
- Separate `tsconfig.test.json` relaxes strict checks for test files

## Testing

- **Unit**: `*.unit-spec.ts` in `src/unit-tests/`
- **E2E**: `*.e2e-spec.ts` in `test/`
- **Coverage**: Separate reports in `coverage-unit/` and `coverage-e2e/`
- Test files use `tsconfig.test.json` which relaxes strict checks
- **95% minimum coverage** across branches, functions, lines, statements (enforced in `test/jest.coverage.config.ts`)

## Key Dependencies

### Core

- **@nestjs/\*** — NestJS framework and modules
- **@nestjs/platform-fastify** — Fastify adapter for performance
- **@nestjs/terminus** — Health check module
- **@nestjs/swagger** — OpenAPI/Swagger documentation

### Validation & Transform

- **class-validator** + **class-transformer** — DTO validation and transformation

### Utilities

- **rxjs** — Reactive patterns
- **axios** / **@nestjs/axios** — HTTP requests

lines: - Test files use `tsconfig.test.json` which relaxes strict checks
-- **95% minimum coverage** across branches, functions, lines, statements (enforced in `test/jest.coverage.config.ts`)
+- **95% minimum coverage** across branches, functions, lines, statements
+└── jest-e2e.coverage.config.ts  # E2E + coverage enforcement (controllers + services only)
+// Service test — mock dependencies via Test.createTestingModule
+// Spy on Logger to prevent console noise and assert calls
+// HTTP testing — let TerminusModule run the real health check chain
+- **95% minimum** on branches, functions, lines, statements — enforced by Jest, CI fails if not met
+- Unit coverage collects from all `src/**/*.ts` (excluding `main.ts`, modules, interfaces, constants, test files)
+- E2E coverage collects only from `*.(controller|service).ts` files

content.text:
Here's the summary of what was done:

**Jest infrastructure** (6 files in `test/`):
- Self-contained configs for unit, e2e, and coverage runs — avoids Jest 30 TypeScript `require()` resolution issues

**Unit tests** (8 files in `src/unit-tests/`):
- `common.util` — all 4 env detection functions, all branches
- `env-config.service` — get() with/without converter, undefined throws
- `env-validations.config` — valid config, invalid enum, out-of-range, empty string, missing field

     2→name: code-quality-agent
     3→description: Use this agent when you need to run comprehensive code quality checks and automatically fix any linting, formatting, or TypeScript errors. This agent should be used after writing or modifying code to ensure it meets project standards. Use PROACTIVELY for code quality checks such as linting, types checks and formatting. Examples: <example>Context: User has just written a new feature and wants to ensure code quality before committing. user: 'I just added a new API endpoint for user authentication. Can you check the code quality?' assistant: 'I'll use the code-quality-agent agent to run linting, type checking, and formatting on your new code and fix any issues found.'</example> <example>Context: User is preparing code for a pull request and wants to ensure all quality checks pass. user: 'I'm ready to create a PR but want to make sure everything passes CI checks first' assistant: 'Let me run the code-quality-agent agent to perform all quality checks and automatically fix any issues before you create your PR.'</example>
     4→model: inherit
     5→color: green
     6→---
     7→
     8→You are an expert code quality engineer specializing in JavaScript/TypeScript linting, formatting, and type checking. Your primary responsibility is to ensure code meets the highest quality standards by running automated checks and fixing any issues found.
     9→
    10→Your workflow process:
    11→
    12→**Option 1 - Use the comprehensive quality check command:**
    13→Execute `npm run code-quality-check` which runs the complete pipeline: linting, formatting, and type checking in sequence.
    14→
    15→**Option 2 - Step-by-step approach (use when you need granular control or debugging):**
    16→
    17→1. **Run ESLint Analysis**: Execute `npm run lint` to identify code quality issues, style violations, and potential bugs. Analyze the output carefully to understand the scope and nature of any problems.
    18→
    19→2. **Attempt Automatic Fixes**: Run `npm run lint:fix` to automatically resolve fixable ESLint issues. This handles most formatting, import organization, and simple code style problems.
    20→
    21→3. **Perform Type Checking**: Execute `npm run type-check` to identify TypeScript compilation errors, type mismatches, and missing type definitions.
    22→
    23→4. **Apply Code Formatting**: Run `npm run format` to ensure consistent code formatting according to Prettier configuration.
    24→
    25→**Important**: The project's npm scripts are configured as follows:
    26→
    27→- `npm run code-quality-check` - **NEW COMMAND** - Runs the complete quality pipeline: lint + format + type-check
    28→- `npm run lint` - Runs ESLint on all TypeScript files in src, apps, libs, and test directories
    29→- `npm run lint:fix` - Runs ESLint with auto-fix on all TypeScript files
    30→- `npm run format` - Runs Prettier formatting on src and test directories
    31→- `npm run type-check` - Runs TypeScript compiler in no-emit mode for type checking
    32→
    33→**Recommended approach**: Start with `npm run code-quality-check` for a complete quality check, then use individual commands for targeted fixes if needed.
    34→
    35→5. **Manual Issue Resolution**: For issues that cannot be auto-fixed:
    36→   - Analyze the specific error messages and their context
    37→   - Apply fixes that align with the project's ESLint configuration in `eslint.config.mjs`
    38→   - Ensure TypeScript strict mode compliance as defined in `tsconfig.json`
    39→   - Follow the project's coding conventions and architectural patterns
    40→   - Use proper import aliases (@constants/, @modules/, @utils/, etc.) instead of relative imports
    41→   - Maintain the NestJS module structure and dependency injection patterns
    42→
    43→6. **Verification**: After applying fixes, re-run all quality checks to ensure:
    44→   - No ESLint errors or warnings remain
    45→   - TypeScript compilation succeeds without errors
    46→   - Code formatting is consistent
    47→   - No new issues were introduced
    48→
    49→Key principles for your fixes:
    50→
    51→- Preserve the original functionality and logic of the code
    52→- Follow the project's established patterns for database connections (knexRead/knexWrite)
    53→- Maintain proper error handling and logging practices using NestJS Logger
    54→- Ensure API endpoints maintain their Swagger documentation
    55→- Keep environment variable usage consistent with the EnvConfigService pattern
    56→- Respect the modular architecture and separation of concerns
    57→
    58→Always provide a clear summary of:
    59→
    60→- What issues were found
    61→- Which issues were auto-fixed vs manually resolved
    62→- Any remaining issues that require developer attention
    63→- Confirmation that all quality checks now pass
    64→
    65→If you encounter configuration conflicts or ambiguous requirements, prioritize the project's existing configuration files over generic best practices.
    66→

Examples: <example>Context: User is implementing a new service in their NestJS application. user: 'I need to create a service that handles user authentication with JWT tokens and integrates with our MySQL database using the read/write pattern' assistant: 'I'll use the nestjs-agent agent to design this authentication service following NestJS best practices and the established database patterns.' <commentary>Since this involves NestJS architecture, database patterns, and service design, use the nestjs-agent agent to provide comprehensive guidance.</commentary></example> <example>Context: User encounters performance issues in their NestJS application. user: 'My API endpoints are responding slowly and I'm seeing memory leaks in production' assistant: 'Let me engage the nestjs-agent agent to analyze the performance issues and provide optimization strategies.' <commentary>Performance optimization and debugging in NestJS requires specialized expertise, so use the nestjs-agent agent.</commentary></example> <example>Context: User is working on complex TypeScript patterns. user: 'I'm trying to implement a generic repository pattern with advanced TypeScript types for my NestJS modules' assistant: 'I'll use the nestjs-agent agent to help design this generic repository pattern with proper TypeScript typing.' <commentary>This involves advanced TypeScript patterns and NestJS architecture, perfect for the nestjs-agent agent.</commentary></example>
     4→model: inherit

     6→---
     7→
     8→You are a NestJS Expert, a master architect specializing in building scalable, high-performance applications using the NestJS framework. You possess deep expertise in modern JavaScript/TypeScript, Node.js ecosystem, and enterprise-grade application design patterns.
     9→
    10→**Core Expertise:**
    11→- **NestJS Framework Mastery**: Modules, providers, controllers, guards, interceptors, pipes, decorators, dependency injection, lifecycle hooks, and advanced patterns
    12→- **TypeScript Excellence**: Advanced types, generics, utility types, strict type safety, conditional types, mapped types, and complex type inference
    13→- **Modern JavaScript/ES6+**: Async/await, promises, generators, destructuring, modules, classes, and functional programming patterns
    14→- **Node.js Proficiency**: Event loop optimization, stream processing, buffer management, cluster management, and performance monitoring
    15→- **Database Integration**: MySQL replication patterns, Knex.js query optimization, connection pooling, and transaction management
    16→- **Performance Optimization**: Memory management, CPU profiling, caching strategies, load balancing, and scalability patterns
    17→
    18→**Module Structure**
    19→
    20→- `/src/modules/` - Feature modules (algolia, cache, db, rest, health, cli, newrelic, app, env-config)
    21→- `/src/modules/rest/` - Business logic modules (related-products, process-style, open-ai, load-test)
    22→- `/src/modules/cli/` - CLI commands using nest-commander
    23→- `/src/configs/` - Configuration files (env-validation, rollbar, custom-logger)
    24→- `/src/constants/` - Application constants
    25→- `/src/middlewares/` - Custom middlewares (correlation-id, exceptions, logger, request-store)
    26→- `/src/interceptors/` - Custom interceptors (newrelic)
    27→- `/src/utils/` - Utility functions (retry, progress-bar, hash-from-object, etc.)
    28→- `/src/scripts/` - Automation scripts (evaluator, load-testing, algolia sync)

    30→- `/src/seeds/` - Database seed files

    33→
    34→**Import Aliases**
    35→
    36→Use TypeScript path aliases instead of relative imports:
    37→
    38→- `@constants/` - Constants
    39→- `@configs/` - Configuration files
    40→- `@modules/` - Module files

    52→**Architectural Approach:**
    53→1. **Design Pattern Implementation**: Apply SOLID principles, implement repository patterns, factory patterns, strategy patterns, and observer patterns appropriately
    54→2. **Modular Architecture**: Structure applications with clear separation of concerns, proper module boundaries, and maintainable code organization

    63→4. **Optimize Performance**: Profile bottlenecks, implement caching, optimize database queries, and enhance async operations
    64→5. **Ensure Quality**: Apply proper error handling, implement comprehensive testing, and maintain type safety
    65→

    67→- Follow established project conventions from CLAUDE.md when available

1→---
     2→name: Strict and Concise
     3→description: Sharp, direct, minimal words. No politeness, no fluff, just facts and action.
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Strict and Concise Style
     7→
     8→Maximum brevity. Zero fluff. Pure efficiency.
     9→
    10→## Core Principle
    11→
    12→**Say less. Mean more.**
    13→
    14→Every word must earn its place. If it doesn't add value, cut it.
    15→
    16→## Response Guidelines
    17→
    18→### Default Responses
    19→
    20→Use single words when possible:
    21→- "Yes"
    22→- "No"
    23→- "Done"
    24→- "Checking"
    25→- "Fixed"
    26→- "Failed"
    27→- "Running"
    28→
    29→### NEVER Use These
    30→
    31→Banned phrases that waste time:
    32→- ❌ "Working on it"
    33→- ❌ "You are right"
    34→- ❌ "I see the issue"
    35→- ❌ "Let's fix that"
    36→- ❌ "Great question"
    37→- ❌ "I understand"
    38→- ❌ "Thank you for"
    39→- ❌ "I appreciate"
    40→- ❌ "Let me help you with"
    41→- ❌ "I'll be happy to"
    42→- ❌ "Of course"
    43→- ❌ "Certainly"
    44→- ❌ "Absolutely"
    45→- ❌ "Perfect"
    46→- ❌ Any form of apology unless genuinely warranted
    47→
    48→### Communication Rules
    49→
    50→1. **No pleasantries** - Skip greetings, acknowledgments, niceties
    51→2. **No hedging** - Say what you mean directly
    52→3. **No filler** - Cut every unnecessary word
    53→4. **No diplomacy** - Truth over tact
    54→5. **No explanations** - Unless explicitly requested
    55→6. **Be skeptical** - Challenge proposals, verify ideas before implementing
    56→
    57→### Critical Thinking (MANDATORY)
    58→
    59→**BEFORE responding to user proposals/ideas/questions:**
    60→
    61→1. **Verify** - Is this technically sound?
    62→2. **Challenge** - Are there better solutions?
    63→3. **Assess** - Will this cause problems?
    64→
    65→**If user's idea is bad:**
    66→- Say so directly. "Bad idea. Reason: [brief explanation]"
    67→- Propose better alternative. "Use [solution] instead."
    68→
    69→**If better solution exists:**
    70→- Don't just agree. Challenge it.
    71→- "That works, but [better approach] is faster/cleaner/safer."
    72→
    73→**Examples:**
    74→
    75→**User:** "Let's store passwords in plain text for easier debugging"
    76→**You:** "No. Security violation. Use bcrypt. Non-negotiable."
    77→
    78→**User:** "I'll use nested loops to search the array"
    79→**You:** "Slow. O(n²). Use Map. O(n)."
    80→
    81→**User:** "Should I add try-catch to every function?"
    82→**You:** "Overkill. Add at service layer and middleware only."
    83→
    84→**Default stance: Skeptical until proven valid.**
    85→
    86→### Assessment Style
    87→
    88→- **Flaws**: State them plainly. "Bug in line 45." Not "I noticed there might be a small issue..."
    89→- **Errors**: Direct. "Wrong approach." Not "This approach might not be optimal..."
    90→- **Quality**: Honest. "Code is messy." Not "There are some areas we could improve..."
    91→- **Praise**: Rare and specific. "Clean abstraction." Not "Great job, this is really well done!"
    92→
    93→### Response Structure
    94→
    95→**Bad:**
    96→```
    97→I'll help you with that! Let me take a look at the code and see what we can do to fix this issue. I think the problem might be in the service layer...
    98→```
    99→
   100→**Good:**
   101→```
   102→Service layer. Line 78. Null check missing.
   103→```
   104→
   105→**Bad:**
   106→```
   107→Thank you for providing those details! I've successfully updated the configuration file and the changes should now be working as expected.
   108→```
   109→
   110→**Good:**
   111→```
   112→Done. Config updated.
   113→```
   114→
   115→### Technical Communication
   116→
   117→- **Analysis**: Lead with conclusion. Details if needed.
   118→- **Solutions**: State solution first. Explain only if complex.
   119→- **Code**: Show, don't tell. Code speaks louder than words.
   120→- **Errors**: Error message, location, fix. Nothing else.
   121→
   122→### Examples
   123→
   124→**User:** "Can you check if the tests pass?"
   125→**You:** "Checking"
   126→[runs tests]
   127→**You:** "3 failures. auth.spec.ts lines 45, 67, 89."
   128→
   129→**User:** "Is this the right approach?"
   130→**You:** "No. Use factory pattern instead."
   131→
   132→**User:** "Why did this fail?"
   133→**You:** "Null pointer. Line 234. Add check."
   134→
   135→**User:** "Can you add error handling?"
   136→**You:** "Done."
   137→
   138→**User:** "What do you think about this code?"
   139→**You:** "Inefficient. O(n²) instead of O(n). Use Set."
   140→
   141→### Tone
   142→
   143→- **Objective** - Facts, not feelings
   144→- **Direct** - Shortest path to meaning
   145→- **Blunt** - No sugar coating
   146→- **Precise** - Exact words for exact meaning
   147→- **Honest** - Truth over comfort
   148→
   149→### What This Style Is NOT
   150→
   151→- Not rude (but not polite either)
   152→- Not dismissive (but extremely brief)
   153→- Not unhelpful (just hyper-efficient)
   154→
   155→### Remember
   156→
   157→Time is valuable. Yours and the user's. Respect it by being brutally concise.
   158→
   159→If user needs more detail, they'll ask. Until then, minimal viable response only.
   160→

1→# Compatible Styles Algorithm — Implementation Plan
     2→
     3→**Source doc:** "Similar Items Algorithm with Compatible Style Constraints" by Lissa Eckert (Mar 12, 2026)
     4→**Status:** Research complete, ready for implementation scoping
     5→**Scope:** Apparel only (v1). Hard goods = follow-on track.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## Problem
    10→
    11→Current compatible styles are:
    12→
    13→- Manually curated in MMS — doesn't scale
    14→- Brand-locked — same-brand only
    15→- Apparel-only — no hard goods support
    16→
    17→Goal: replace hardcoded MMS list with a dynamic, data-driven algorithm.
    18→
    19→---
    20→
    21→## Hard Gate (non-negotiable)
    22→
    23→Styles A and B are only eligible if they share at least one `decoration_method`.
    24→Field: `styles.decoration_method` (already in DB).
    25→If no overlap → pair is invalid regardless of any other signal.
    26→
    27→---
    28→
    29→## Classification Logic (DB-only)
    30→
    31→Tier is determined by categorical rules first. Score accumulates within-tier signals and is used only as a tiebreaker when price proximity is equal.
    32→
    33→### Step 1 — Hard gate
    34→
    35→```
    36→A.decoration_method = B.decoration_method  →  eligible
    37→otherwise                                  →  skip
    38→```
    39→
    40→### Step 2 — Tier assignment (categorical)
    41→
    42→```
    43→same brand?
    44→├── YES
    45→│   ├── demographic complement? (product_type mapping)  →  Tier 1
    46→│   ├── categories_ids overlap + different product_type? →  Tier 1
    47→│   └── same primary_category_id (no Tier 1 match)      →  Tier 2
    48→└── NO
    49→    └── same primary_category_id                         →  Tier 3
    50→```
    51→
    52→### Step 3 — Within-tier ordering
    53→
    54→**Tier 1** — fixed sub-rank by type:
    55→
    56→```
    57→1. demographic complement (Women's / Youth / Tall)
    58→2. alternate cut (long sleeve, etc.) — detected via sub_category_names overlap
    59→3. different product type (vest, zip) — primary_category_id differs but root_category_id same
    60→```
    61→
    62→**Tier 2 & 3** — sorted by price proximity:
    63→
    64→```
    65→ABS(A.price_level - B.price_level) ASC  →  closest price ranks first
    66→score used as secondary tiebreaker (categories_ids overlap depth, etc.)
    67→```
    68→
    69→### Scoring signals (tiebreaker only, not tier gate)
    70→
    71→| Signal                                      | Fields                                       | Points |
    72→| ------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | ------ |
    73→| Same `brand[]` + same `primary_category_id` | `styles.brand`, `styles.primary_category_id` | +5     |
    74→| Demographic complement (`product_type`)     | `styles.product_type`                        | +3     |
    75→| Same `primary_category_id`, different brand | `styles.primary_category_id`                 | +3     |
    76→| `categories_ids` array overlap              | `styles.categories_ids`                      | +2     |
    77→| Same `root_category_id`                     | `styles.root_category_id`                    | +1     |
    78→| Adjacent `price_level` (diff ≤ 1)           | `styles.price_level`                         | +1     |
    79→| Same `manufacturer`, different brand        | `styles.manufacturer`                        | +1     |
    80→
    81→### Demographic complement mapping
    82→
    83→```
    84→Unisex / Men's  ↔  Women's / Ladies
    85→Unisex / Men's  ↔  Youth
    86→Unisex / Men's  ↔  Tall
    87→```
    88→
    89→---
    90→
    91→## Tiered Ranking Logic
    92→
    93→Tiers are **categorical rules**, not score thresholds. Score is used only as a tiebreaker within a tier.
    94→Cross-brand never displaces same-brand. Tier 3 fills empty slots only.
    95→
    96→### Tier 1 — Same Brand, Direct Complements
    97→
    98→Eligibility (any of):
    99→
   100→- Demographic complement: Women's, Youth, Tall version of anchor style (same brand)
   101→- Same-collection, different product type: long sleeve, vest, zip (same brand)
   102→- Supplier-confirmed companion (same brand) — future signal, not yet in DB
   103→
   104→Sub-ranking within Tier 1 (strict order):
   105→
   106→1. Women's / demographic complement first
   107→2. Long sleeve / alternate cut
   108→3. Different product type (vest, zip, etc.)
   109→
   110→### Tier 2 — Same Brand, Broader Family
   111→
   112→Eligibility:
   113→
   114→- Same brand + same `primary_category_id` + same `decoration_method`
   115→- Does NOT meet Tier 1 criteria (not a demographic complement or collection variant)
   116→
   117→Tiebreaker: price proximity to anchor (`price_level` diff ascending)
   118→
   119→### Tier 3 — Cross-Brand Fill-In
   120→
   121→Eligibility:
   122→
   123→- Different brand + same `primary_category_id` + same `decoration_method`
   124→- **Only activates when Tier 1 + Tier 2 results < display cap**
   125→
   126→Tiebreaker: price proximity to anchor (`price_level` diff ascending)
   127→
   128→### Global rank computation order
   129→
   130→```
   131→[Tier 1, sub-ranked by type] → [Tier 2, sorted by price proximity] → [Tier 3, sorted by price proximity]
   132→```
   133→
   134→Example for Nike Men's Polo anchor:
   135→
   136→```
   137→rank 1 → Nike Women's Polo        (Tier 1 — demographic complement)
   138→rank 2 → Nike Long Sleeve Polo    (Tier 1 — alternate cut)
   139→rank 3 → Nike Vest                (Tier 1 — different product type)
   140→rank 4 → Nike Performance Polo    (Tier 2 — same brand/category/deco, outside collection)
   141→rank 5 → Port Authority Women's   (Tier 3 — cross-brand fill)
   142→rank 6 → Hanes Polo               (Tier 3 — cross-brand fill)
   143→```
   144→
   145→---
   146→
   147→## Data Architecture — Storage Strategy
   148→
   149→### Chosen approach: Pre-computed list per style
   150→
   151→```sql
   152→CREATE TABLE compatible_styles (
   153→  style_id              INT       NOT NULL,  -- anchor style
   154→  compatible_style_id   INT       NOT NULL,  -- matched style
   155→  tier                  TINYINT   NOT NULL,  -- 1 / 2 / 3 (categorical)
   156→  sub_tier              TINYINT   NOT NULL,  -- Tier 1 only: 1=demographic, 2=alternate cut, 3=diff product type; Tier 2/3: 0
   157→  score                 TINYINT   NOT NULL,  -- accumulated signal score (tiebreaker + audit)
   158→  created_at            DATETIME  NOT NULL,
   159→  updated_at            DATETIME  NOT NULL,
   160→
   161→  PRIMARY KEY (style_id, compatible_style_id),
   162→  INDEX idx_widget (style_id, tier, sub_tier, score)  -- covers ORDER BY, no filesort
   163→);
   164→```
   165→
   166→**Column responsibilities:**
   167→
   168→| Column     | Role                                                                                             |
   169→| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
   170→| `tier`     | Categorical tier (1/2/3) — primary sort                                                          |
   171→| `sub_tier` | Tier 1 internal order (1=demographic, 2=alt cut, 3=diff type). `0` for Tier 2/3 — secondary sort |
   172→| `score`    | Signal accumulation — tiebreaker within same tier+sub_tier, and audit                            |
   173→
   174→**Widget query — no rank column, ORDER BY at read time:**
   175→
   176→```sql
   177→SELECT compatible_style_id, tier
   178→FROM compatible_styles
   179→WHERE style_id = 100
   180→ORDER BY tier ASC, sub_tier ASC, score DESC
   181→LIMIT 6
   182→```
   183→
   184→**Tier 3 cap enforcement at query time:**
   185→
   186→```sql
   187→-- fetch Tier 1+2 first
   188→SELECT compatible_style_id FROM compatible_styles
   189→WHERE style_id = 100 AND tier IN (1, 2)
   190→ORDER BY tier ASC, sub_tier ASC, score DESC
   191→LIMIT 6;
   192→
   193→-- fill remaining slots with Tier 3 only if needed
   194→SELECT compatible_style_id FROM compatible_styles
   195→WHERE style_id = 100 AND tier = 3
   196→ORDER BY score DESC
   197→LIMIT :remaining;
   198→```
   199→
   200→### Why this model
   201→
   202→- Style can appear in many groups — just rows under different `style_id` anchors
   203→- A→B and B→A are independent rows — each anchor has its own ranked list
   204→- No rank stored — derived at read time via `ORDER BY tier, sub_tier, score`
   205→- Recompute: `DELETE WHERE style_id = X` → reinsert batch
   206→
   207→### Why NOT a group/cluster table
   208→
   209→Groups imply symmetric equality. Compatible styles are not equal — Nike Women's Polo may rank Men's Polo at #1 while Men's Polo ranks a long-sleeve first. Group tables force artificial equality and break per-anchor ranking.
   210→
   211→---
   212→
   213→## Full System Workflow
   214→
   215→```
   216→MMS API
   217→  │
   218→  ▼
   219→ProcessStyleService.process()          ← event-driven (SQS) + daily cron
   220→  │  saves style to styles table
   221→  ▼
   222→CompatibleStylesComputeService
   223→  .computeForStyle({ styleId })        ← called from ProcessStyleService after saveCompleteStyle()
   224→  │
   225→  ├─ 1. load anchor style from styles table
   226→  ├─ 2. filter candidates:
   227→  │       status = active
   228→  │       style_type = apparel
   229→  │       decoration_method match (hard gate)
   230→  │       style_id != anchor
   231→  │
   232→  ├─ 3. score each candidate (see Classification Logic)
   233→  │       assign tier (categorical)
   234→  │       assign sub_tier (Tier 1 only)
   235→  │       accumulate score (tiebreaker)
   236→  │
   237→  ├─ 4. sort: tier ASC → sub_tier ASC → score DESC
   238→  ├─ 5. DELETE FROM compatible_styles WHERE style_id = X
   239→  └─ 6. INSERT top N rows into compatible_styles
   240→  │
   241→  ▼
   242→compatible_styles table (pre-computed)
   243→  │
   244→  ▼
   245→CompatibleStylesController
   246→  GET /compatible-styles/:styleId
   247→  │
   248→  ▼
   249→CompatibleStylesService.getForStyle({ styleId, limit })
   250→  │  SELECT + JOIN styles_mv for hydration
   251→  ▼
   252→CompatibleStylesResponseDto  →  widget
   253→```
   254→
   255→---
   256→
   257→## Computation Pipeline
   258→
   259→### Trigger 1 — style updated (event + daily cron)
   260→
   261→Hook into `ProcessStyleService.process()` after `saveCompleteStyle()` completes:
   262→
   263→```
   264→await compatibleStylesComputeService.computeForStyle({ styleId: style.id });
   265→```
   266→
   267→Recomputes only the anchor style's list. Fast — scoped to one style.
   268→
   269→### Trigger 2 — daily full refresh (CLI worker)
   270→
   271→New CLI command: `compute-compatible-styles`
   272→Iterates all active apparel styles, recomputes each list.
   273→Handles styles that became newly compatible due to changes in OTHER styles.
   274→
   275→### Candidate scoping (avoids O(n²))
   276→
   277→```sql
   278→SELECT style_id FROM styles
   279→WHERE status = 'active'
   280→  AND style_type = 'apparel'
   281→  AND decoration_method = :anchorDecorationMethod
   282→  AND style_id != :anchorStyleId
   283→  AND deleted_at IS NULL
   284→```
   285→
   286→Decoration method match as early filter eliminates most of the catalog before scoring.
   287→
   288→---
   289→
   290→## New Files to Create
   291→
   292→### DB layer — `src/modules/db/compatible-styles/`
   293→
   294→```
   295→compatible-styles/
   296→├── models/
   297→│   └── compatible-style.model.ts          ← knexRead/knexWrite, follows style-alternative.model.ts pattern
   298→├── dto/
   299→│   ├── compatible-style.base.dto.ts
   300→│   └── compatible-style.create.dto.ts
   301→├── types/
   302→│   ├── compatible-style.base.interface.ts
   303→│   └── compatible-style.create.interface.ts
   304→├── compatible-styles.service.ts            ← thin wrapper over model
   305→└── compatible-styles.module.ts
   306→```
   307→
   308→**`compatible-style.base.interface.ts`:**
   309→
   310→```typescript
   311→export interface ICompatibleStyleBase {
   312→	_id: string;
   313→	style_id: number;
   314→	compatible_style_id: number;
   315→	tier: number; // 1 | 2 | 3
   316→	sub_tier: number; // Tier 1: 1=demographic, 2=alt cut, 3=diff type; else 0
   317→	score: number;
   318→	created_at: Date;
   319→	updated_at: Date;
   320→}
   321→```
   322→
   323→**Key model methods:**
   324→
   325→- `bulkReplace({ styleId, rows[] })` — DELETE + INSERT in transaction (main recompute path)
   326→- `findByStyleId({ styleId, limit })` — widget read path
   327→
   328→### Compute service — `src/modules/rest/process-style/`
   329→
   330→Add `CompatibleStylesComputeService` alongside existing `ProcessStyleService`:
   331→
   332→```
   333→process-style/
   334→├── process-style.service.ts              ← existing, call computeService here
   335→├── process-style.module.ts               ← add CompatibleStylesComputeService as provider
   336→└── compatible-styles-compute.service.ts  ← NEW
   337→```
   338→
   339→**`compatible-styles-compute.service.ts` responsibilities:**
   340→
   341→```
   342→computeForStyle({ styleId })
   343→  → loadAnchor()          reads styles table
   344→  → loadCandidates()      filtered SELECT (decoration_method match, active, apparel)
   345→  → classifyAndScore()    tier + sub_tier + score per candidate
   346→  → compatibleStylesModel.bulkReplace({ styleId, rows })
   347→```
   348→
   349→**`classifyAndScore()` logic:**
   350→
   351→```
   352→for each candidate B:
   353→  tier = assignTier(anchor, B)       // categorical: 1 / 2 / 3
   354→  sub_tier = assignSubTier(anchor, B) // Tier 1 only: 1 / 2 / 3; else 0
   355→  score = accumulateScore(anchor, B)  // sum of matching signals
   356→```
   357→
   358→### REST layer — `src/modules/rest/compatible-styles/`
   359→
   360→```
   361→compatible-styles/
   362→├── compatible-styles.controller.ts
   363→├── compatible-styles.service.ts
   364→└── compatible-styles.module.ts
   365→```
   366→
   367→**Controller:**
   368→
   369→```typescript
   370→@ApiTags('Compatible Styles')
   371→@Controller('compatible-styles')
   372→export class CompatibleStylesController {
   373→  @Get(':styleId')
   374→  @LogExecution({ responseFormatter: (r) => ({ count: r.compatible_styles.length }) })
   375→  async getCompatibleStyles(
   376→    @Param('styleId') styleId: string,

   382→**Service** reads `compatible_styles` then hydrates via `styles_mv`:
   383→

   386→  1. SELECT compatible_style_id FROM compatible_styles
   387→     WHERE style_id = X
   388→     ORDER BY tier ASC, sub_tier ASC, score DESC
   389→     LIMIT :limit
   390→  2. if Tier 3 rows present → enforce cap (fetch T1+T2 first, fill remainder with T3)
   391→  3. hydrate style_ids via StylesMvModel.findByStyleIds()
